title: Africa's genomics paradox: zero pharmacogenomics dose-finding for the world's most genetically diverse continent
primary_estimand: Count of genomics / precision medicine / pharmacogenomics interventional trials in Africa vs comparator regions
certainty: high (registry data, complete enumeration within ClinicalTrials.gov)
body: Does Africa, the continent with the greatest human genetic diversity, host precision medicine trials proportionate to its genomic significance? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 for interventional studies matching genomics, precision medicine, pharmacogenomics, whole genome, exome, or genetic testing, stratified by continent and country of trial location. Africa returned one hundred and twenty-four broadly genomics-related trials versus five thousand one hundred and seventeen in the United States, a ratio of roughly one to forty-one. Gene-specific pharmacogenomics dose-finding trials for CYP2D6, CYP2B6, CYP3A5, UGT1A1, VKORC1, and HLA-B returned zero in Africa versus fifty-five in the United States. The H3Africa genomics consortium produced zero registered interventional trials, confirming a complete pipeline failure from sequencing to clinical translation. BRCA and biomarker-driven trials numbered eighty-one in Africa versus over two thousand in the United States, despite higher triple-negative breast cancer prevalence in African women. The analysis is limited to one registry and cannot capture trials listed on African or WHO platforms.
ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used in early drafting. The final text was rewritten by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.

checks.value: Gene-specific PGx dose-finding: 0 in Africa across all 6 genes; H3Africa: 0 interventional trials
Africa 124 broad vs US 5117 (1:41); PGx: Africa 0 vs US 55; BRCA: Africa 81 vs US 2021
H3Africa consortium yielded no registered interventional trials
CYP2D6/CYP2B6 at 2-3x frequency, zero local dose-finding
81 in Africa vs 2021+ in US despite higher TNBC prevalence
single registry, cannot capture African/WHO platform registrations
